A peer-reviewed study in PMC states the 1815 Tambora eruption, the largest historically observed eruption, cooled global land temperatures in 1816 by an estimated 1.9 degrees C (plus or minus 0.2 degrees C), and that the European summer of 1816 has been described as the 'year without a summer', followed by a 'year of famine' in 1817.

published.description: In April 1815, Mount Tambora on Sumbawa Island in present-day Indonesia produced the largest volcanic eruption in recorded history, rated 7 on the Volcanic Explosivity Index, with explosions peaking on April 10-11 and leaving a caldera 6 km across. Pyroclastic flows killed thousands in the surrounding region, and the ash and sulfate aerosols injected into the stratosphere cooled global land temperatures by an estimated 1.9 degrees C in 1816 - the 'Year Without a Summer' - causing crop failures and famine across the Northern Hemisphere. Counting the resulting starvation and disease, it is the deadliest volcanic eruption in recorded history.

impact.summary: The USGS lists 92,000 deaths for the 1815 Tambora eruption - the deadliest volcanic eruption since 1500 CE - with starvation as the major cause; that point figure is carried here. Other authoritative accountings differ because indirect famine and disease mortality is hard to bound: a separate USGS article states more than 70,000 died in the surrounding areas, while NOAA reports 11,000 killed by pyroclastic flows plus more than 100,000 from the resulting food shortages over the following decade, implying a total above 111,000.

location.region: Mount Tambora, Sumbawa Island, Lesser Sunda Islands; climate effects worldwide

published.name: Mount Tambora Eruption

claims.text: USGS states the 1976 magnitude 7.8 earthquake which struck Tangshan, China killed somewhere between 250,000 and 800,000 people.

A USGS publication reports the Tangshan earthquake killed, by official count, 240,000 people, and that during the first 2 years after the earthquake estimates of casualties ranged from 650,000 to 800,000, adding that almost certainly the exact number will never be known.

USGS records the Tangshan earthquake as a magnitude 7.8 event that occurred on July 28, 1976 at 3:42 a.m. Beijing local time, with a focal depth of 15 kilometers, causing serious damage and loss of life in the densely populated industrial city.

A peer-reviewed study of historical earthquakes in China (1950-2017) published via NIH/PMC states the 1976 Ms 7.8 Tangshan earthquake caused 242,000 deaths, the deadliest earthquake in China in that 68-year period, accounting for 68.7% of all Chinese earthquake deaths studied.

published.description: On July 28, 1976, at 3:42 a.m. local time, a magnitude 7.8 earthquake with a focal depth of about 15 kilometers struck the densely populated industrial city of Tangshan in Hebei Province, China. Striking without recognized warning while most residents slept, it flattened much of the city and ranks among the deadliest earthquakes in recorded history. China's official count put the dead at roughly 240,000, while early estimates ran as high as 800,000.

impact.summary: USGS states the earthquake killed somewhere between 250,000 and 800,000 people. China's official count was about 240,000 dead (Chinese studies cite 242,000), but USGS notes that estimates of casualties during the first two years after the earthquake ranged from 650,000 to 800,000 and that the exact number will almost certainly never be known.

location.region: Tangshan, Hebei Province, North China

published.name: 1976 Tangshan Earthquake

claims.text: In total, six million Jewish men, women, and children were murdered by the Nazi German regime and its allies and collaborators, per the US Holocaust Memorial Museum.

The US Holocaust Memorial Museum defines the Holocaust as the systematic, state-sponsored persecution and murder of six million European Jews by the Nazi German regime and its allies and collaborators, occurring between 1933 and 1945.
Nazi Germany also murdered around 3.3 million Soviet prisoners of war and around 1.8 million non-Jewish Poles, per the US Holocaust Memorial Museum.
At least 250,000, but possibly as many as 500,000, Roma were murdered under Nazi persecution, per the US Holocaust Memorial Museum.

published.description: The Holocaust was the systematic, state-sponsored persecution and murder of six million European Jews by the Nazi German regime and its allies and collaborators between 1933 and 1945. Killing escalated to industrialized genocide after 1941 through mass shooting operations, killing centers such as Auschwitz-Birkenau, and lethal conditions in ghettos and camps. Millions of others — including Soviet prisoners of war, non-Jewish Poles, Roma, Serb civilians, and people with disabilities — were also murdered under Nazi persecution.

impact.summary: The US Holocaust Memorial Museum states that six million Jewish men, women, and children were murdered by the Nazi German regime and its allies and collaborators; exact counts are unknowable and six million is the documented scholarly consensus figure rather than a precise tally. This figure covers Jewish victims only — the Holocaust as defined by USHMM. Nazi persecution and mass murder also killed millions of others, including around 3.3 million Soviet prisoners of war, around 1.8 million non-Jewish Poles, at least 250,000 (possibly as many as 500,000) Roma, more than 310,000 Serb civilians, and 250,000–300,000 people with disabilities.

location.region: Europe, centered on Germany, occupied Poland, and the occupied Soviet Union

published.name: The Holocaust

claims.text: The Smithsonian Global Volcanism Program states that Santorini's youngest caldera formed about 3,600 years before present (around 1600 BCE) during the Late Bronze Age 'Minoan' eruption, which forced abandonment of the island.

Per the Smithsonian Global Volcanism Program, excavations at Akrotiri show a Minoan city buried by the catastrophic Late Bronze Age eruption about 3,500 years ago: strong earthquakes prompted residents to evacuate between the first minor ash falls and the devastating eruption, after which the city was partially buried by heavy falls of ash and pumice and then overtopped by pyroclastic flows.

NOAA's NCEI Global Historical Hazard Database records the Santorini eruption at 1610 BCE with a Volcanic Explosivity Index (VEI) of 7 and notes that deaths probably occurred on Thera and, as a result of tsunamis, in ports and seaside villages on Crete and elsewhere, but that 'no direct evidence of deaths has been found'; it lists literature dates for the eruption ranging from the late 17th century BCE to 1380 BCE.

NOAA states that the explosive eruption of the volcanic island of Santorini (Thera) in the Aegean Sea around 1610 BCE generated a tsunami that swept the shores of nearby islands and contributed to the end of the Minoan culture on the nearby island of Crete.

published.description: The Late Bronze Age 'Minoan' eruption of Thera (Santorini) in the Aegean Sea, around 1600 BCE, was one of the largest volcanic events of the past several millennia (VEI 7), collapsing the island's youngest caldera and forcing abandonment of the island. The eruption buried the Minoan town of Akrotiri under ash, pumice, and pyroclastic flows and generated a tsunami that swept the shores of nearby islands, contributing to the end of Minoan culture on Crete.

impact.summary: The death toll is unquantified. NOAA's NCEI Global Historical Hazard Database states that deaths probably occurred on Thera and, as a result of tsunamis, in ports and seaside villages on Crete and elsewhere, but that no direct evidence of deaths has been found; excavations at Akrotiri indicate residents evacuated the city between the first minor ash falls and the devastating eruption. The eruption's date is itself uncertain, with published estimates ranging from the late 17th century BCE (radiocarbon, e.g. 1627-1600 BCE) to c. 1380 BCE in older literature.

location.region: Santorini (Thera), Cyclades islands, Aegean Sea; tsunami effects on Crete and nearby Aegean shores

published.name: Minoan Eruption of Thera (Santorini)

claims.text: The Battle of Luetzen (1632), a major engagement of the Thirty Years' War, killed between 6,000 and 9,000 soldiers, and the Swedish king Gustavus II Adolphus was mortally wounded on the battlefield; the war continued for another 16 years after this battle (ending in 1648).

Genomic evidence indicates that plague (Yersinia pestis) circulated from Germany to France and Italy following Thirty Years' War troop movements; one sequenced genome (BRA001) came from the remains of a Swedish soldier who occupied Brandenburg, Germany, in 1631 during the war.

Total mortality from the Thirty Years' War is commonly estimated at roughly 4.5 to 8 million people, the majority from famine and epidemic disease rather than combat; Encyclopaedia Britannica frames the loss as an overall population decline of about 15-20% in the Holy Roman Empire (from about 20 million to 16-17 million).

published.description: The Thirty Years' War (1618-1648) was a series of interconnected conflicts fought mainly in the German lands of the Holy Roman Empire, beginning as a struggle between Catholic and Protestant states and drawing in most major European powers, including Spain, France, Sweden, Denmark, and the Dutch Republic. It was one of the most destructive conflicts in European history, with the great majority of deaths caused by famine and epidemic disease rather than combat. The war ended with the Peace of Westphalia in 1648.

impact.summary: Widely cited estimates put total mortality from the Thirty Years' War at roughly 4.5 to 8 million soldiers and civilians, with the great majority of deaths caused by famine and epidemic disease (plague, typhus, dysentery) rather than by combat. The figure is inherently uncertain: it rests on fragmentary early-modern records and is often expressed instead as a population decline of about 15-20% across the Holy Roman Empire (from roughly 20 million to 16-17 million), with some hard-hit regions such as Wuerttemberg losing more than half their people. This overall range could not be confirmed on a readable allowlisted page during research (Encyclopaedia Britannica and JSTOR returned HTTP 403 to automated fetches, and the relevant NIH/PMC and Cambridge journal articles are scanned page images); it is therefore recorded here as an unverified estimate, while battle- and epidemic-level details below are verified from allowlisted sources actually read.

location.country: Germany (Holy Roman Empire) and Central Europe
location.region: Principally the German lands of the Holy Roman Empire, with fighting extending into Bohemia and adjoining regions; combatants included Spain, France, Sweden, Denmark, and the Dutch Republic.

published.name: Thirty Years' War

claims.text: NOAA's NCEI Global Historical Hazard Database records the 79 CE Vesuvius eruption (volcano event 1348) with a Volcanic Explosivity Index (VEI) of 5 and 2,100 deaths, noting that about 2,000 skeletons were found in partly-excavated Pompeii and hundreds more in partly-excavated Herculaneum, and that pyroclastic surges affected these and other towns and produced most of the fatalities with the total unknown; it cites Luongo et al. (2003) for about 1,150 identified victims in Pompeii (about 1,600 including estimates for unexcavated areas) and about 300 persons killed by pyroclastic surges in shoreline boathouses at Herculaneum.

A peer-reviewed bioarchaeological and palaeogenomic study of two Pompeian victims, available through PubMed Central (NIH), states that more than 2,000 individuals died as a direct consequence of the 79 AD Vesuvius eruption, 'the deadliest ever in European history', and gives Pompeii a population ranging between 6,400 and 20,000 dwellers.

A peer-reviewed study of the 79 AD victims, available through PubMed Central (NIH), reports that at Herculaneum about 300 people who had taken refuge in waterfront chambers were suddenly engulfed by the collapse of the rapidly advancing first pyroclastic surge, that the populations at risk were about 4,000–5,000 at Herculaneum and about 20,000 at Pompeii, and that later pyroclastic surges and flows reached up to 30 kilometers from Vesuvius, with victims found even 20 kilometers away at Stabiae.

A peer-reviewed study of the deadly thermal impact of the 79 CE ash-cloud surges at Herculaneum, available through PubMed Central (NIH), reports that about 350 people were discovered in the fornici (beachfront chambers) with only a few dozen victims found in the town proper, and estimates temperatures of 555–495 °C for the first pyroclastic surge that reached the town.

published.description: In 79 CE Mount Vesuvius erupted explosively (VEI 5) over the Bay of Naples, burying the Roman towns of Pompeii and Herculaneum — along with Stabiae, Oplontis, and other settlements — under plinian pumice fall and pyroclastic surges hot enough to kill instantly. The eruption is traditionally dated to 24 August, though the record also carries a late-October dating, and peer-reviewed studies describe it as the deadliest volcanic eruption in European history. The buried cities, rediscovered in the 18th century, preserve an unparalleled archaeological snapshot of Roman life.

impact.summary: NOAA's NCEI Global Historical Hazard Database records 2,100 deaths — about 2,000 skeletons found in partly-excavated Pompeii plus hundreds more at Herculaneum, including roughly 300 in shoreline boathouses — while explicitly noting that pyroclastic surges produced most of the fatalities and the total is unknown. Peer-reviewed studies likewise state that more than 2,000 individuals died as a direct consequence of the eruption. Because both cities are only partly excavated and an unknown number died outside them (victims were found as far as Stabiae, about 20 km away), the true toll is presumed higher; popular accounts cite estimates up to roughly 16,000, but that upper figure could not be verified against any allowlisted source and is not adopted here.

location.region: Bay of Naples, Campania — Pompeii, Herculaneum, Stabiae, Oplontis and surrounding settlements

published.name: Eruption of Mount Vesuvius (79 CE)

claims.text: An estimated 9 million combatants and as many as 7 million civilians died in the First World War (1914-1918), according to a peer-reviewed article in Emerging Infectious Diseases hosted on NIH's PubMed Central.
World War I claimed an estimated 16 million lives, according to the U.S. National Archives exhibit on the influenza epidemic of 1918.
More than 7 million soldiers died in World War I, according to Our World in Data's 'War and Peace' entry.

published.description: World War I (1914-1918), also called the First World War or the Great War, was a global conflict between the Allied Powers (led by France, the United Kingdom, Russia, and later the United States) and the Central Powers (led by Germany, Austria-Hungary, and the Ottoman Empire). Fought mainly on the Western and Eastern Fronts in Europe, with additional campaigns in the Middle East, Africa, and at sea, it introduced industrialized trench warfare, chemical weapons, tanks, and aerial combat. The war ended with the Armistice of 11 November 1918 and redrew the map of Europe and the Middle East, dissolving four empires.

impact.summary: A peer-reviewed article hosted on NIH's PubMed Central states that an estimated 9 million combatants and as many as 7 million civilians died in the war, implying a total toll of roughly 9 to 16 million; the U.S. National Archives independently cites an estimated 16 million lives claimed. Military-death estimates in the scholarly literature cluster around 8.5-10 million, while civilian tolls are far more uncertain (some reference works put war-attributable civilian deaths well above 7 million, depending on whether famine, disease, massacres, and the overlapping 1918 influenza pandemic are counted).

location.region: Western and Eastern Fronts in Europe; additional theaters in the Middle East, Africa, the Balkans, and the Atlantic

published.name: World War I

claims.text: A peer-reviewed study (Kesternich et al., 'The Effects of World War II on Economic and Health Outcomes across Europe') states that between 62 and 78 million people died due to WWII, out of a 1939 world population of approximately 2 billion, with 39 million deaths in Europe alone.

The US Holocaust Memorial Museum's Holocaust Encyclopedia states that World War II resulted in an estimated 55 million deaths worldwide, beginning with Germany's invasion of Poland on September 1, 1939.
Our World in Data states that in World War II alone, more than 21 million combatants died, making it far deadlier than most conflicts.

The US Holocaust Memorial Museum documents that six million Jewish men, women, and children were murdered by the Nazi German regime and its allies and collaborators during the Holocaust, alongside millions of non-Jewish victims including approximately 3.3 million Soviet prisoners of war and around 1.8 million ethnic Poles.

published.description: The Second World War (1939-1945) was a global conflict between the Allied powers and the Axis powers (Germany, Italy, and Japan), fought across Europe, Asia, the Pacific, North Africa, and the Atlantic. It began with Germany's invasion of Poland on 1 September 1939 and ended with Germany's unconditional surrender in May 1945 and Japan's surrender in September 1945. It is the deadliest conflict in human history, encompassing the Holocaust, strategic bombing of cities, and the first use of nuclear weapons.

impact.summary: Total deaths are estimated only as wide ranges because wartime record-keeping collapsed across both Allied and Axis states. A peer-reviewed study by Kesternich, Siflinger, Smith, and Winter states that between 62 and 78 million people died due to WWII (over 3% of the 1939 world population of roughly 2 billion), with 39 million deaths in Europe alone; the US Holocaust Memorial Museum gives a lower estimate of about 55 million deaths worldwide. The toll includes more than 21 million combatant deaths (Our World in Data) and the six million Jews murdered in the Holocaust; roughly half of European casualties were civilians.

location.region: Europe, Soviet Union, East and Southeast Asia, Pacific, North Africa, and the Atlantic

published.name: Second World War
